prompt: For ٱلْبَيْتُ كَبِيرٌ, name the sentence type and its two parts.
expected_answer: Nominal. ٱلْبَيْتُ = mubtadaʾ (the house, definite, with الْ); كَبِيرٌ = khabar ([is] big, indefinite, tanwīn). No verb, so supply is; the usual shape is a definite subject and an indefinite predicate.
accepted_variants: nominal: mubtadaʾ ٱلْبَيْتُ + khabar كَبِيرٌ, supply is
definite subject, indefinite predicate, no copula
forbidden_answers: there is a hidden verb is in the Arabic

concept: verbal sentence is verb-first; doer follows the verb
prompt: For ذَهَبَ ٱلْوَلَدُ, name the type and label the parts.
expected_answer: Verbal. ذَهَبَ = fiʿl (went, Form I past, root ذ ه ب); ٱلْوَلَدُ = fāʿil (the boy) following the verb. English reorders to the boy went; Arabic is verb-first.
accepted_variants: verbal: fiʿl ذَهَبَ + fāʿil ٱلْوَلَدُ after the verb
verb-first, doer follows
forbidden_answers: the doer comes before the verb in Arabic

concept: case endings tell doer (ḍamma) from object (fatḥa)
prompt: For كَتَبَ ٱلطَّالِبُ ٱلدَّرْسَ, label the parts and say which case marks the doer and which the object.
expected_answer: Verbal. كَتَبَ fiʿl; ٱلطَّالِبُ fāʿil (the student, ḍamma = nominative doer); ٱلدَّرْسَ mafʿūl bihi (the lesson, fatḥa = accusative object). The case endings tell doer (ḍamma) from object (fatḥa); read them.
accepted_variants: fāʿil ḍamma = doer, mafʿūl fatḥa = object
read the case endings to assign doer/object
forbidden_answers: word order alone fixes doer and object
required_reasoning: parts labelled by case not by order

concept: a detached pronoun heads a nominal sentence
prompt: For هُوَ غَفُورٌ, name the type and its parts.
expected_answer: Nominal. هُوَ = mubtadaʾ (He); غَفُورٌ = khabar ([is] forgiving). A detached pronoun can head a nominal sentence; still no verb.
accepted_variants: nominal: mubtadaʾ هُوَ + khabar غَفُورٌ
detached pronoun as subject, no verb
forbidden_answers: there is a copula verb in the Arabic
required_reasoning: no verb between subject and predicate

concept: jār-majrūr as the predicate of a nominal sentence
prompt: For ٱلْكِتَابُ فِي ٱلْبَيْتِ, name the type and the predicate, and the case فِي assigns.
expected_answer: Nominal. ٱلْكِتَابُ = mubtadaʾ; the predicate is the jār-majrūr فِي ٱلْبَيْتِ (in the house) standing in for is in the house. فِي puts ٱلْبَيْتِ in jarr (kasra).
accepted_variants: nominal: mubtadaʾ + jār-majrūr predicate; فِي governs jarr
the PP فِي ٱلْبَيْتِ is the khabar

concept: iḍāfa is one noun phrase; a muḍāf takes no الْ
prompt: For بَابُ ٱلْبَيْتِ مَفْتُوحٌ, name the mubtadaʾ and one rule about the muḍāf.
expected_answer: Nominal. The mubtadaʾ is the iḍāfa بَابُ ٱلْبَيْتِ (the door of the house): two nouns, the second in jarr, and no الْ on the first because a muḍāf cannot take الْ. مَفْتُوحٌ = khabar ([is] open). Treat the iḍāfa as a single noun phrase.
accepted_variants: mubtadaʾ = iḍāfa بَابُ ٱلْبَيْتِ; muḍāf takes no الْ
the construct is one unit, second noun in jarr
forbidden_answers: بَابُ takes الْ as a muḍāf
the two nouns are separate sentence parts

concept: إِنَّ governs naṣb on its ism
prompt: For إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ, what case does ٱللَّهَ take and why, and is the fatḥa an object marker?
expected_answer: Nominal, headed by إِنَّ (indeed). After إِنَّ the subject ٱللَّهَ takes naṣb (fatḥa) as the ism inna, and غَفُورٌ is the khabar inna ([is] forgiving). The fatḥa is governed by إِنَّ, not an object marker.
accepted_variants: إِنَّ puts the ism in naṣb; fatḥa is from إِنَّ not an object
ism inna in naṣb, khabar inna غَفُورٌ
forbidden_answers: ٱللَّهَ is the object so it takes fatḥa
the fatḥa is an object marker
required_reasoning: إِنَّ named as governing the case
naṣb on the ism attributed to إِنَّ
fatḥa not misread as an object marker

concept: مَا as negation decided by the following past verb frame
prompt: For مَا ذَهَبَ ٱلْوَلَدُ, what does مَا contribute and why is that reading frame-dependent?
expected_answer: Verbal, negated. مَا = negation (did not), ذَهَبَ fiʿl, ٱلْوَلَدُ fāʿil → the boy did not go. مَا reads as negation here because a past verb follows it in a declarative frame; مَا is multi-function, so confirm the frame.
accepted_variants: مَا = did not here, because a past verb follows in a declarative frame
negation reading is frame-dependent for multi-function مَا
required_reasoning: frame named as deciding the negation reading

concept: verb mood decides لَا prohibition vs negation
prompt: For لَا تَحْزَنْ, what kind of لَا is it and what decides it?
expected_answer: Verbal, prohibition. لَا + a jussive (majzūm) verb تَحْزَنْ (sukūn ending) = do not grieve. Had the verb been indicative (لَا تَحْزَنُ), لَا would be plain negation (you do not grieve). The mood of the verb decides which لَا you have.
accepted_variants: prohibition; jussive verb → لَا prohibition, indicative → negation
the verb's mood (jussive vs indicative) decides لَا
required_reasoning: لَا function tied to the mood

concept: a leading question particle frames the clause, is not the subject
prompt: For هَلْ تَعْلَمُ, what is هَلْ and what is its role?
expected_answer: Verbal, interrogative. هَلْ = a yes/no question marker (do…?); تَعْلَمُ = verb (you know) → do you know? The leading particle frames the whole clause as a question; it is not the subject.
accepted_variants: هَلْ = yes/no question marker, frames the clause, not the subject
interrogative particle, not a sentence part like subject
forbidden_answers: the particle has no grammatical role

concept: relative clause modifies a noun; relative agrees with referent
prompt: For ٱلرَّجُلُ ٱلَّذِي عَلِمَ, name the structure and what ٱلَّذِي agrees with.
expected_answer: Nominal noun phrase. ٱلرَّجُلُ (the man) + ٱلَّذِي (relative pronoun, who) + عَلِمَ (knew) → the man who knew. ٱلَّذِي links a clause as a modifier and agrees in gender and number with its referent.
accepted_variants: relative ٱلَّذِي links a modifying clause, agrees with its referent
the man who knew; relative agrees in gender/number
forbidden_answers: agreement with the referent does not matter
عَلِمَ is the main predicate of the man

concept: a conditional is marked by its two jussive verbs
prompt: For مَنْ يَعْمَلْ خَيْرًا يَجِدْهُ, name the structure and the mark of the conditional pair.
expected_answer: Conditional. مَنْ (whoever) + the condition verb يَعْمَلْ (jussive) + object خَيْرًا (good), then the answer verb يَجِدْهُ (jussive, + object pronoun it) → whoever does good will find it. Both verbs are jussive, the mark of a conditional pair.
accepted_variants: both verbs jussive = the conditional pair; مَنْ whoever
condition + answer, both majzūm

concept: a prepositional phrase still needs an attachment; pending when undecided
prompt: For جَاءَ بِكِتَابٍ, what is بِكِتَابٍ, and what do you write if the frame does not decide its attachment?
expected_answer: Verbal. جَاءَ = fiʿl, and بِكِتَابٍ is a jār-majrūr attached to the verb frame; the bāʾ is not decoration and the host is not enough by itself. If a PP could attach as a predicate, hāl, ṣifa, or hidden complement and the frame does not decide, write pending: pp_attachment.
accepted_variants: jār-majrūr on the verb; if attachment is undecided write pending: pp_attachment
name the attachment or mark PP attachment pending
forbidden_answers: guess an attachment when the frame is silent
